# ARC-QUIC-CRT-0006 - QUIC Transport Bridge, Diagnostics, and Sender/Recovery Ownership Architecture

## Purpose

Describe the main-library seams that keep TLS-facing transport facts, diagnostics emission, and sender/recovery ownership inside Incursa.Quic instead of the interop harness.

## Scope

This slice stays in the main library because it owns transport/runtime facts that any future host could reuse. The interop harness only consumes the seam later.

## Requirements Satisfied

- REQ-QUIC-CRT-0103
- REQ-QUIC-CRT-0104
- REQ-QUIC-CRT-0105

## Design Summary

The library adds three adjacent seam types. A TLS bridge state captures transport-visible facts such as local and peer transport parameters, key availability, handshake confirmation, key updates, key discard, and fatal alerts. The connection reducer threads those bridge outputs into transport-parameter commits, handshake latching, and explicit close handling while the connection send runtime absorbs Initial and Handshake discard cleanup. A diagnostics sink abstraction keeps emitters format-agnostic and cheap or no-op when disabled. A sender/recovery runtime owner remains available as the broader transport-layer helper surface for packet-sent state, PTO ownership, and retransmission planning.

## Key Components

- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicTransportTlsBridgeState.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicConnectionSendRuntime.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicDiagnostics.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicSenderRecoveryRuntime.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicConnectionRuntime.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicRecoveryTiming.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicCongestionControlState.cs

## Risks

- The new seam classes must stay architectural helpers rather than becoming a second TLS or recovery implementation.
- Diagnostics plumbing should remain opt-in so the library still works cleanly in non-interop hosts.
